sandboxFiles.name: [NTK] Project Ollie Eng Design
[UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"

sandboxFiles.name: CodeMender PRD.doc
sandboxFiles.content:
# Product Requirement Document (PRD): CodeMender

## 1. Executive Summary
CodeMender is an autonomous, agentic AI code security system designed to scan, verify, and remediate deep software vulnerabilities. Built on top of a highly optimized agent platform and powered by Google's frontier LLMs (such as Gemini), CodeMender moves beyond passive scanning. By executing proof-of-concept exploits in safe, sandboxed environments and generating functional code patches, CodeMender eliminates false positives, mitigates developer fatigue, and accelerates the overall time-to-remediation (TTR).

## 2. Background and Objectives
Modern development organizations face an overwhelming volume of security findings from static analysis tools. Security teams struggle to differentiate between theoretical risks and active, exploitable vulnerabilities, while developers lack the security expertise or bandwidth to write and test reliable patches. 

CodeMender solves this bottleneck through three primary objectives:
* **Reduce False Positives:** Verify vulnerability exploitability in isolated execution environments before flagging them for human review.
* **Automate Remediation:** Provide developers with verified, contextual, and language-accurate code fixes rather than generic security advice.
* **Maintain Enterprise Control:** Ensure all scanning, exploitation, and patching happen within user-controlled, isolated sandbox environments with strict data privacy guarantees.

## 3. Target Personas
* **Security Engineers / DevSecOps:** Responsible for defining code security policies, managing vulnerability pipelines, and prioritizing high-blast-radius threats. They need to integrate CodeMender with tools like Wiz and orchestrate scanning at scale.
* **Software Developers:** Tasked with fixing vulnerabilities. They need frictionless integrations (e.g., isolated git branches and Pull Requests) and high-quality, regression-tested code patches.
* **Enterprise Security Leadership (CISO/VP of Security):** Needs assurance that customer source code is never used for external model training, that security costs are manageable, and that compliance standards (VPC-SC, FedRAMP, etc.) are strictly maintained.

## 4. Core Functional Requirements

### 4.1. Vulnerability Detection (Scan)
* **LLM-Based Static Scanning:** CodeMender must autonomously scan raw code repositories to find deep vulnerabilities related to authentication, access control, injection attacks, memory safety, deserialization, and logical flaws.
* **3rd-Party Import Integration:** Support direct ingestion of vulnerability reports from 3P static analysis tools (e.g., Wiz) and fuzzing suites to act as a target list for validation and remediation.
* **Vulnerability Export:** Enable exporting scanned security findings back to enterprise vulnerability management graphs (such as Wiz's security graph) for centralized blast-radius and vulnerability-chain analysis.

### 4.2. Exploit Verification (Verify)
* **Proof-of-Concept (PoC) Generation:** For any detected or imported vulnerability, the agent must attempt to write an executable test script (`exploit.sh`) to safely trigger and verify the vulnerability.
* **Isolated Sandbox Execution:** CodeMender must run the generated exploit in a remote, isolated sandbox or user-configured container environment. 
* **Browser Limitations:** If a vulnerability relies strictly on front-end browser behavior that cannot be accessed or mocked programmatically, the agent must log this limitation and bypass automated UI verification.
* **Artifact Generation:** The verification process must output the following local artifacts for developer observability:
    * `exploit.sh`: The script executing the exploit in the remote environment.
    * `LOG.md`: Detailed logs of the agent's actions during the verification phase.
    * `PLAN.md`: The logical plan constructed by the agent to run and test the exploit.
    * `REPORT.md`: A comprehensive report detailing the vulnerability, reproduction steps, a Mermaid diagram illustrating the attack flow, and recommended fix strategies.

### 4.3. Automated Remediation (Fix)
* **Patch Generation:** Provide semantic, functional code patches matching the host repository’s programming language, framework, and stylistic patterns.
* **Contextual Inputs:** Allow developers to pass custom hints, application constraints, or specific security guidelines during scanning, verification, or fixing using the `-c` flag (supporting inline strings or raw markdown files).
* **Regression Mitigation:** Recommend and support local integration testing to ensure generated security patches do not break existing business logic.

## 5. Technical Requirements & Integrations

### 5.1. Language and Framework Support
The system must support major enterprise development environments out of the box:
* **Languages:** C/C++, Go, Java, Python, TypeScript/JavaScript, Rust, Ruby.
* **Frameworks:** Standard language libraries, Django, Flask, React, Spring Boot, and Express.

### 5.2. CI/CD Pipeline Integration
CodeMender is designed to operate within containerized, ephemeral CI/CD pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Google Cloud Build). To prevent credential exposure and maintain state across isolated runs, the pipeline must implement:
* **Identity and Authentication:** Pipeline runners must authenticate to backend Google Cloud APIs via Workload Identity Federation (OAUTH), assuming a Google Cloud Service Account without hardcoding long-lived secrets.
* **State Persistence:** Since CI/CD run environments are ephemeral, the runner must pull the active state (encryption keys, identity tokens, previous scan metrics) from a secure Google Cloud Storage (GCS) bucket at the beginning of a run, and write back updated states upon run completion.
* **Workflow Automation:** Support a two-phase execution model:
    1. Scan and report findings back to the repository.
    2. Apply verified fixes on isolated git branches and automatically open Pull Requests for human review.

### 5.3. Model Orchestration
* **Multi-Model Support:** Customers must have the flexibility to swap underlying models based on speed, cost, and complexity requirements. Supported models for Public Preview:
    * Gemini 3.5 Flash (Default)
    * Gemini 3.1 Pro
    * Gemini 3 Flash
* **Model Configuration:** Users must be able to change the default model selection by modifying the local `~/.codemender/config.yaml` file.

## 6. Security, Compliance, and Data Privacy

### 6.1. Security Guardrails
* **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL):** By default, CodeMender must prompt for explicit user approval before executing any read/write actions on the host file system (e.g., reading/writing files, creating new directories). 
* **Bypass Settings:** Advanced users can bypass the HITL requirement by configuring authorization parameters directly in their local `~/.codemender/config.yaml` file.

### 6.2. Data Privacy & Retentions
* **No Foundation Model Training:** Customer source code, prompt structures, intermediate artifacts, and vulnerability reports processed by CodeMender must never be used to train Google’s public foundation models.
* **Local Artifact Isolation:** All security artifacts, logs, plans, and patch recommendations must be returned and stored locally on the client's host machine. Google will not persist source code, prompts, or findings server-side.
* **Minimal Telemetry:** Server-side logs are restricted to platform health metrics (e.g., service availability, execution latency, error rates) and must never contain proprietary code or metadata.
* **Secure Routing:** Support customer-configured VPC Service Controls (VPC-SC) to route CLI and agent traffic entirely within Google's private network backbone.

### 6.3. Compliance Milestones
* **Public Preview:** Full VPC-SC support.
* **Fast Follow:** Data Redundancy Zones (DRZ), Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK), and Access Transparency.
* **Future Roadmap:** Sovereign cloud compliance, IL5, and FedRAMP certification.

## 7. Pricing and Licensing Model
Billing is calculated based on token usage and charged under a dual-SKU model:
* **SKU 1: Base Model SKU:** Charged at standard PayGo rates per million input/output tokens (differentiated by the chosen model: Gemini 3.5 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Pro, or Gemini 3 Flash).
* **SKU 2: CodeMender Premium SKU:** A 100% premium surcharge applied on top of the base model token rate.
* **Discounting:** CodeMender premium SKUs will align with GenAIv2 enterprise discounting frameworks.

## 8. Release Roadmap
* **Private Preview:** Completed. Limited to ~10 high-touch enterprise customers.
* **Public Preview (July 21, 2026):** Global endpoint availability. Support for Gemini 3.5 Flash, 3.1 Pro, and 3 Flash. VPC-SC compliance active.
* **General Availability (GA):** Timeline TBD. Planned additions include UI integration, Gemini 3.5 Pro support, Opus 4.7 integration, automated severity re-scoring, and deeper IDE integrations.
